title: Social Values Map

introduction.subtitle: Four axes of social tension — mapped without ideological labels. Inspired by the 8values project.
introduction.description:
This is not a quiz that puts you in a political box. It maps the value tensions you carry — places where two goods pull against each other. The result describes tensions, not identities.

State how much you agree with each statement. There are no right answers; both poles of every axis have a use.

Use the result as language for reflection and conversation, not as a permanent label. The terms are meant to make patterns easier to notice, compare, and discuss.
introduction.duration: 24 statements. Approximately three to four minutes.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is your choice.
introduction.disclaimer: Inspired by the 8values project (MIT). Items are original and not from the 8values quiz. This is not a validated political assessment.

questions.text: People who work harder and smarter deserve to earn more than those who do not.

conceptHelp.title: Equality ↔ Merit: People who work harder and smarter deserve to earn more than those who do not.
conceptHelp.summary: Equality ↔ Merit means the tension between reducing unequal outcomes and rewarding individual effort, talent, or contribution. This statement is one small behavioral signal for that pattern: "People who work harder and smarter deserve to earn more than those who do not.".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Self-knowledge items are useful when they help you notice repeated behavior. They become misleading if one answer is treated as a permanent identity or diagnosis.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Think across normal weeks, not one unusually good or bad day. Rate the statement by how often it describes your real preference or behavior.
conceptHelp.example: A high answer means this statement is regularly true for you; a low answer means it is rare, context-specific, or not how you usually operate.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
